channel.title: Comments for page ""Baby's First Guide to Euclid-Class Anomalies" & Other Questionable Documents Recovered from the Hard Drive of Dr. ████ ███████"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread ""Baby's First Guide to Euclid-Class Anomalies" & Other Questionable Documents Recovered from the Hard Drive of Dr. ████ ███████"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creepy. I like it. I'm wondering, though, since no one else mentioned this in the comments, am I the only one who got a nonconsensual vibe from this? It seems like a cognitohazard that brainwashes you into a relationship, so… or is the implication supposed to be that affected researchers are lonely enough to choose not to reject a relationship with an anomalous being despite the mission of the Foundation, rather than that the anomaly makes them accept the relationship?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's creative. I love how the picture changed as well. You really got me then.
1+ (Sorry for late comment)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry for months-late post. Yeah, this is pretty spot-on, though I do have to admit the Series listing title with waifu in it is fully a joke from chat.
An unusual article with an equally unusual title, but one easily deserving of the score. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a little confusing isn't it? As best as I understand it (from both the article and the comments):

1) AWCY? created a piece of artwork that acts as a cognition hazard, but only those who are trained to deal with those hazards are affected by it. Those with no knowledge of the cognition hazards' properties are not effected… (Almost like a double cognition hazard or a reverse cognition hazard).

2)The effect of the image is that those who are exposed to it believe they are in a relationship with a person/ entity. In Japanese culture, many men do not have time for relationships (that or relationships are more stress than they'd like at the time) so they find affection from games that simulate romantic partners. Waifus do not get disappointed in when you come home late from work, don't get mad when you forget to start the dishwasher, etc. Those exposed believe they are in a relationship like that.

If I am wrong I would love some help understanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmmm,good point… So the whole shtick is just one big cognitohazard then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think somebody should show the pages,or rather make up what the other six pages were…:/

Taken straight from the article:

all files save for the title page (footnote: six other unreadable .png files were found in addition to the attached image) are corrupted beyond readability, and are unable to display.

That's why they're not there. Speaking out-of-universe, they're not shown in the article because they're not the focus of it. I was considering making an entire other book like I did in the first installment, but I figured it'd get gimmicky fast and detract from the actual content of SCP-2708.

If your comment means "someone should make it for fun" (the "somebody should show the pages" and the :/ bit are what led me to think you were saying that you're disappointed with it not being in the actual article) then sure! That's fine by me. But it's not in the article itself for the above reason(s).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think somebody should show the pages,or rather make up what the other six pages were…:/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the implication I'm getting is "a memetic family"

Not necessarily. Remember that we don't know whether or not the entity, SCP-2708-A, actually exists - even anomalously/non-corporeally - or is strictly a hallucination brought on by the baseline cognitohazardous effects tied to viewing Figure A. Or maybe it's both, because what is reality? Is reality what we perceive or what others perceive? If you yourself feel and see something as being real, and you believe it's real, couldn't you argue that it is real, even if observers disagree? Are the baseline parameters of reality personal or public? Things like that.

So no, it's not an entire family by concept, it's the effect of convincing oneself that one has a partner and is able to proceed down typical life paths with said partner (yes, including making a family, in that one instance) - but it can only go so far, and it depends on the extent to which the person affected believes the events to be true. That's why amnesticization usually fixes the problem, if not removing the conceptual memories entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm not the sharpest knife in the drawer, but the implication I'm getting is "a memetic family". Which is worth a +1. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one thing I want to point out about this is how it uses AWCY. They're anartists, but usually, we aren't looking at them as people who actually think about their art. We usually see dangerous people trying to make a point, cause a panic, and say, "that's art!" That has the same effectiveness as spreading hate and then saying, "But I'm only joking!"

But here, we don't have that. We have someone who is frustrated with the natural problem in art, namely how do you craft a message and ensure that it is effectively received? A viewer's interpretation of the art is fully dependent on the viewer's experiences, and so the artist's emotional thrust can be utterly lost to the viewer. Every author here, whether their articles succeed or get deleted, knows this struggle.

So can that be subverted? Must the artwork end at the viewer's judgment of it? Or can the judgment of that art become part of the art itself? Can the artist elicit the feeling in the viewer directly, and not rely on ever changing cultural markers and interpretations and experiences inherent in people the artist will never meet or know to effect its impact? This is one anartist's attempt to answer that question.

And that is how you ask if we're cool yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure the payoff is quite worth it (I may have just misread something in the final addendum), but there's good build up to "Figure A", the moving images are unnerving, and packing it all behind this "Baby's First" thing is kind of neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I see what you mean; I was hoping to just imply that it would theoretically work for someone who's, say, aromantic but not asexual, rather than go into the details of the aro/ace parameters ("I'm aromantic but feel sexual attraction" "I'm asexual but feel romantic attraction" "I don't feel either" etc.) but I can see how it's seen as an error. Let me read that paragraph again and see if there's a way I can clarify it without making it all too wordy.

Edit: Alright, edited to be a little more specific. Thank you for your help!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got some confusion with the anomaly criteria. If being capable of sexual and/or romantic attraction is what triggers it, then it would only be avoidable by subjects incapable of both, but the footnote indicates that either would be sufficient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought that one tale might be a one-off joke, but this adds a tear jerking new side to the "Book".
/me clutches heart ;A;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this is the good strangeness that keeps me coming back. Or going back. Whatever, back is involved ( hat tip, Aiden Eldritch).

Cyantreuse, bravo or brava as the case may be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This in itself is damn good. The fact that it makes its predecessor much more tragic, makes it even better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Baby's First Guide got back

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded:
Unless otherwise stated, any graphics or images in my articles are made and/or taken by me.

This is me getting away from all the skips in my sandbox I'm supposed to be finishing. It's also a coldpost, so I'm just risking it as it is. But I'd been wanting to explain Baby's Guide for a while, so here we go.

Edit: I think I know what tags go on this, but I'll let someone on the tag team do it just to be safe.

Edit 2: I have the SCPper Chrome extension and it's saying that this is a translated page and the original is a page called "undefined," which is super weird.

Edit 3: By the way, Figure A is actually a redacted version of a tattoo design I designed yesterday, so in a few weeks I'll have a cognitohazard on my lower back.
